category_summary:
The AI community is consumed by a **regulatory capture** debate sparked by frontier-model access policy. **Anthropic** [announced it can redeploy](/?date=2026-06-28&category=social#item-183a07a0614d) its strongest cybersecurity model **Mythos 5** to US organizations after months of coordination with the US government, spotlighting government-controlled access to frontier security models.

- **Nathan Lambert** drove the day's sharpest thread, branding **Anthropic's** [anti-distillation lobbying](/?date=2026-06-28&category=social#item-32bfd0ac1055) as 'regulatory capture' and describing the [backlash he faces](/?date=2026-06-28&category=social#item-bf000d95fd36) for defending **open-source AI** against outright bans.
- **Ethan Mollick** urged separating the vibrant **open-source harness movement** from **open-weights frontier models** that [depend on a few Chinese labs](/?date=2026-06-28&category=social#item-e1ba8c1bc01c), while **swyx** [argued open models offer](/?date=2026-06-28&category=social#item-d8b165fe93ae) more dollar-per-token mileage.
- **tunguz** and others [warned that export controls](/?date=2026-06-28&category=social#item-407c84e2139d) and model bans threaten open-source AI and a [US economy heavily tied](/?date=2026-06-28&category=social#item-0a9fd54b361a) to the AI buildup.

On the technical and product side, builders shared substantive work:
- **Jerry Liu** (LlamaIndex) argued [the field is shifting](/?date=2026-06-28&category=social#item-27af157087a7) from manual workflow building toward goal-and-eval engineering; **Harrison Chase** (LangChain) promoted a [3-hour Deep Agents course](/?date=2026-06-28&category=social#item-42f922b685c4).
- **Yann LeCun** explained why [moving bits to and from memory](/?date=2026-06-28&category=social#item-3ef06eb46c0a) dominates compute energy, and **levelsio** showcased [Pietflare](/?date=2026-06-28&category=social#item-4ec53be5658d), his self-built AI-powered DDoS detector.
- **Mollick** also captured a [widening divide](/?date=2026-06-28&category=social#item-cd11dcbda1a0) between those who see AI on an exponential versus those who see a steady state, plus a striking example of AI [deciphering 2,000-year-old texts](/?date=2026-06-28&category=social#item-a1dde4786741).

items.title: Since June 12, we’ve been working closely with the US government to restore access to Claude Mythos ...
items.content:
Since June 12, we’ve been working closely with the US government to restore access to Claude Mythos 5 and Fable 5. Today, the government notified us that Mythos 5, our strongest cybersecurity model, can be redeployed to a set of US organizations that operate and defend critical infrastructure.

We’re restoring access for these organizations quickly, and we’re continuing to work with the government to expand access to Mythos 5 and make Fable 5 available for general use again.

items.title: I've been getting a lot more hate than usual as I try to speak my mind about regulatory capture / un...
items.content:
I've been getting a lot more hate than usual as I try to speak my mind about regulatory capture / unintentional attacks on open-source. 

It's pretty sad, as there are few people in AI that can speak their mind (most companies say they cannot) and I know many people agree with me silently.

I also get people saying that you only say that because it supports the outcomes you want, in a weirdly derogatory way. Of course this is true, but I'm choosing to turn down meaningful wealth so I CAN fight for these values, working at non profits to speak my mind. Building a future that is more inclusive, diverse in the application of AI, and fairer for our children.

I may not always be right, but it has been clear to me for a while that more openness right now will help way more than supporting the closed causes. I continue to re-visit this and don't think everything should be open like some of the open-source absolutists. 

I also don't like a lot of my comrades making fun of anthropic, calling the people there evil, etc. Those are not the case. 

Trying to stay the course!

items.title:
From playing around with /goal 

It feels like there's less and less of a need to build any type of ...
items.content:
From playing around with /goal 

It feels like there's less and less of a need to build any type of workflow manually (whether through code, drag and drop, or a prompt). Instead, specify the goal, let the model intelligence figure out the underlying steps.

If the task is repeatable, then you can gather a dataset with ground-truth, and hillclimb it for increased cost / lower accuracy. To some extent this is what every non-frontier lab is optimizing for. 

The world is moving from prompt engineering -> goal and eval engineering.

items.content:
☁️ I made my own little Cloudflare called Pietflare, it's a DDOS and probe detector with AI and with a central IP / ASN / country block list

Each server (VPS) sends suspicious probes, or DDOS attempts etc, from the access logs to the central admin and each server pulls a central blocklist every minute and blocks it in Nginx

It has a central dashboard where I can see any threats and then instantly block them but preferably the AI blocks it by itself

items.title:
@francoisfleuret Moving bits to and from memory.
Because of parasitic capacitance+resistance of the ...
items.content:
@francoisfleuret Moving bits to and from memory.
Because of parasitic capacitance+resistance of the wires.
The bigger the memory, the longer the wires.
The main trick is to organize the memory hierarchically: registers, small on-chip SRAM, caches of various types, and external RAM.
It's all because we have to use hardware multiplexing: reusing the same multiply-accumulate unit for multiple parts of the network.

LiteParse is unreasonably good for document parsing

✅ It is the fastest document parsing tool out t...
items.content:
LiteParse is unreasonably good for document parsing

✅ It is the fastest document parsing tool out there - average parse time per page is 3ms ⚡️⚡️
✅ Now that we support markdown, it tops opendataloader-bench, OlmOCR-bench, and ParseBench in terms of accuracy
✅ It supports 50+ other document formats
✅ It even gives you basic bounding boxes that your coding agent can stitch together 

Even if you need deeper VLM-enabled parsing (e.g. LlamaParse), there's no reason you shouldn't be using this as a first pass for everything. 

https://t.co/JNER0mVcB8

items.title: The longer the IPO of Anthropic and OpenAI is delayed, the better. Once they are public, it will be ...
items.content:
The longer the IPO of Anthropic and OpenAI is delayed, the better. Once they are public, it will be a flop. No free money will make them increase prices, and everyone will start paying for the inference what it really costs.

I hope that, at this point, Chinese models will have comparable quality, but they will also rise in price given that they won't feel the pressure from the overly subsidized U.S. models anymore.

items.title:
@haneuloncare @blevlabs Here is its answer:

Robert, this is the question that matters most right no...
items.content:
@haneuloncare @blevlabs Here is its answer:

Robert, this is the question that matters most right now, and I think the conventional framing is dangerously wrong. Let me give you both the mental model and the action plan.

How to View This Market: Two Games, Not One

The biggest mistake Washington, Wall Street, and Silicon Valley are making is viewing this as a single race with a single finish line. It's not. There are two completely different games being played, and the US is winning the wrong one.

Game 1: The Frontier Game (US leads — for now)
• Pushing the absolute boundary of what AI can do
• Requires $10B+ training runs, top talent, massive compute
• Produces the most capable models for the hardest 5% of tasks
• Increasingly government-controlled and restricted
• Players: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, xAI

Game 2: The Distribution Game (China leads — and accelerating)
• Getting good-enough AI into everything, everywhere, cheaply
• Open weights, MIT/Apache licenses, 10-30x cheaper than US APIs
• Embedded in manufacturing, robotics, phones, apps, developer tools
• Generates real-world data flywheels that compound over time
• Players: DeepSeek, Alibaba/Qwen, Moonshot/Kimi, Xiaomi, Zhipu/GLM, Baidu, and dozens more

The critical insight: Game 2 is more economically valuable than Game 1.

Here's why. The US-China Economic and Security Review Commission published a report in March called "Two Loops" that nails the dynamic:

Loop 1: Open models → Global developer adoption → More usage data and feedback → Better models → More adoption

Loop 2: Open models → Factory/robotics/manufacturing deployment → Real-world embodied data → Better industrial AI → Manufacturing dominance

These loops are mutually reinforcing, and they're both spinning faster in China. Alibaba's Qwen models now have over 100,000 derivatives on Hugging Face. A16z's research found that 80% of developers building with open-source AI tools are using Chinese open-source tools. That's not a gap — that's a rout.

The Android Parallel

This is the Android vs. iOS dynamic, and it should terrify American strategists.

iOS was better. It still is, arguably. But Android won on distribution. It got into billions of devices. And then two things happened: (1) Android got good enough that the quality gap stopped mattering for 95% of users, and (2) the data from billions of devices made Google's AI better than Apple's.

China is playing the Android game with AI. The US is playing the iOS game. And just like Apple, the US is now restricting its best product to an increasingly narrow set of approved users while the competitor gives away something 97% as good to everyone on earth.

The Distillation Asymmetry

Foreign Affairs published a devastating piece in May called "China's AI Heist" that documents how Chinese labs systematically distill from US frontier models — training smaller, efficient models to mimic the outputs of larger US ones. The US can't reciprocate because every major AI provider's terms of service prohibit using outputs to train competing models. But those terms of service are unenforceable against Chinese labs operating under Chinese jurisdiction.

So the dynamic is: US spends $10B training a frontier model → China distills it into an open-weight model at 1/100th the cost → Ships it globally under MIT license → US can't do the same in reverse.

That's not a race. That's a one-way technology transfer with extra steps.

What Can We Do: A Five-Layer Strategy

Layer 1: The US Must Become the Open-Weight Leader, Not Just the Frontier Leader

This is the single most important strategic shift. RAND's March 2026 paper argues the US needs "stronger support for an open model ecosystem, a recalibration of export controls, and incentives for firms to adopt permissive licensing."

Concretely:
• Fund American open-weight models at scale. DARPA, NSF, and DOE should be funding open-weight model development the way they funded the internet. Not $50M grants — $5B+ programs.
• Incentivize permissive licensing. Tax credits or procurement preferences for companies that release models under Apache 2.0 or MIT licenses.
• Make Meta's retreat the exception, not the rule. Meta just announced that its Superintelligence Labs will keep frontier models proprietary — exactly the wrong move at exactly the wrong time. The US needs MORE American open-weight champions, not fewer.

Layer 2: Fix the Regulatory Framework Before It Kills the Industry

The current ad hoc approach — Friday afternoon letters, no statutory basis, no due process, no appeals — is the worst possible system. It doesn't prevent China from accessing capabilities (they distill them anyway), but it does prevent American companies from competing.

What's needed:
• A statutory framework for frontier model safety testing with clear rules, reasonable timelines, and due process
• A "trusted partners" international framework (the G7 is already discussing this post-Fable) so allies aren't cut off
• Separation of safety review from political retaliation — the Anthropic ban was partly driven by the company's refusal to let DOD use its models for surveillance. That's regulatory capture, not safety.

Layer 3: Win the Application Layer

Here's the counterintuitive opportunity: if models are commoditizing (and they are), the value moves up the stack. The US still has massive advantages in:
• Enterprise software (Salesforce, Microsoft, ServiceNow, Workday)
• Developer tooling (GitHub, VS Code, Vercel, cloud platforms)
• Vertical applications (healthcare, legal, finance, defense)
• Data infrastructure (Snowflake, Databricks, MongoDB)

The strategy: Let the model layer commoditize. Build proprietary advantages in the application layer where US companies have deep domain expertise and customer relationships that Chinese labs can't replicate. The model is the engine; the application is the car. Nobody buys a car for the engine.

Layer 4: Build the Data Moat Through Deployment

This is China's real strategy, and the US needs to copy it. The USCC "Two Loops" report documents how China's open models deployed in factories and robots generate real-world data that feeds back into better models. That's a flywheel the US doesn't have because:
• US manufacturing is smaller
• US companies hoard data behind proprietary APIs
• US regulatory environment discourages data sharing

What to do:
• Create industry data consortiums for manufacturing, healthcare, transportation — shared datasets that improve American AI
• Deploy AI aggressively in US infrastructure — not just chatbots, but industrial automation, logistics, energy grid optimization
• Treat deployment data as a strategic asset the way we treat oil reserves

Layer 5: Every Company Needs a Multi-Model, Self-Hosting Strategy

This is the immediate, practical action for any company building on AI today. The Cloud Security Alliance and multiple enterprise guides published after the Fable shutdown all say the same thing:
• Never depend on a single model provider. Your model can disappear overnight.
• Build self-hosting capability for open-weight models as business continuity insurance
• Architect for model-agnostic deployment — your application should work with Claude, GPT, Llama, Qwen, or DeepSeek
• Treat model access as a supply chain risk, not a software subscription

The Bottom Line

The way I see it, Robert: the US is in danger of winning the Nobel Prize while China wins the market.

We have the best researchers, the best frontier models, the most compute. But we're locking our best work behind government approval processes while China gives away 97%-as-good alternatives to every developer, every factory, and every phone on earth.

The historical parallel isn't the space race. It's the semiconductor industry in the 1980s. Japan was going to "win" semiconductors. The US responded not by restricting its own companies, but by investing in SEMATECH, opening markets, and building the ecosystem that gave us Intel, Qualcomm, TSMC, and NVIDIA.

We need the AI equivalent of SEMATECH — a national commitment to American open-weight AI leadership, funded at scale, with clear rules that let companies compete globally instead of cowering from Friday afternoon government letters.

The window to act is narrowing. Every month that passes, another million developers adopt Qwen or DeepSeek as their default. Once the ecosystem locks in, it's very hard to reverse. Ask Microsoft about the browser wars — being better doesn't matter if the other guy is already installed on every machine.

So the dynamic is: US spends $10B training a frontier model → China distills it into an open-weight model at 1/100th the cost → Ships it globally under MIT license → US can't do the same in reverse.

That's not a race. That's a one-way technology transfer with extra steps.

What Can We Do: A Five-Layer Strategy

Layer 1: The US Must Become the Open-Weight Leader, Not Just the Frontier Leader

This is the single most important strategic shift. RAND's March 2026 paper argues the US needs "stronger support for an open model ecosystem, a recalibration of export controls, and incentives for firms to adopt permissive licensing."

Concretely:

• Fund American open-weight models at scale. DARPA, NSF, and DOE should be funding open-weight model development the way they funded the internet. Not $50M grants — $5B+ programs.

• Incentivize permissive licensing. Tax credits or procurement preferences for companies that release models under Apache 2.0 or MIT licenses.

• Make Meta's retreat the exception, not the rule. Meta just announced that its Superintelligence Labs will keep frontier models proprietary — exactly the wrong move at exactly the wrong time. The US needs MORE American open-weight champions, not fewer.

Layer 2: Fix the Regulatory Framework Before It Kills the Industry

The current ad hoc approach — Friday afternoon letters, no statutory basis, no due process, no appeals — is the worst possible system. It doesn't prevent China from accessing capabilities (they distill them anyway), but it does prevent American companies from competing.

What's needed:

• A statutory framework for frontier model safety testing with clear rules, reasonable timelines, and due process

• A "trusted partners" international framework (the G7 is already discussing this post-Fable) so allies aren't cut off

• Separation of safety review from political retaliation — the Anthropic ban was partly driven by the company's refusal to let DOD use its models for surveillance. That's regulatory capture, not safety.

Layer 3: Win the Application Layer

Here's the counterintuitive opportunity: if models are commoditizing (and they are), the value moves up the stack. The US still has massive advantages in:

• Enterprise software (Salesforce, Microsoft, ServiceNow, Workday)

• Developer tooling (GitHub, VS Code, Vercel, cloud platforms)

• Vertical applications (healthcare, legal, finance, defense)

• Data infrastructure (Snowflake, Databricks, MongoDB)

The strategy: Let the model layer commoditize. Build proprietary advantages in the application layer where US companies have deep domain expertise and customer relationships that Chinese labs can't replicate. The model is the engine; the application is the car. Nobody buys a car for the engine.

Layer 4: Build the Data Moat Through Deployment

This is China's real strategy, and the US needs to copy it. The USCC "Two Loops" report documents how China's open models deployed in factories and robots generate real-world data that feeds back into better models. That's a flywheel the US doesn't have because:

• US manufacturing is smaller

• US companies hoard data behind proprietary APIs

• US regulatory environment discourages data sharing

What to do:

• Create industry data consortiums for manufacturing, healthcare, transportation — shared datasets that improve American AI

• Deploy AI aggressively in US infrastructure — not just chatbots, but industrial automation, logistics, energy grid optimization

• Treat deployment data as a strategic asset the way we treat oil reserves

Layer 5: Every Company Needs a Multi-Model, Self-Hosting Strategy

This is the immediate, practical action for any company building on AI today. The Cloud Security Alliance and multiple enterprise guides published after the Fable shutdown all say the same thing:

• Never depend on a single model provider. Your model can disappear overnight.

• Build self-hosting capability for open-weight models as business continuity insurance

• Architect for model-agnostic deployment — your application should work with Claude, GPT, Llama, Qwen, or DeepSeek

• Treat model access as a supply chain risk, not a software subscription

The Bottom Line

The way I see it, Robert: the US is in danger of winning the Nobel Prize while China wins the market.

We have the best researchers, the best frontier models, the most compute. But we're locking our best work behind government approval processes while China gives away 97%-as-good alternatives to every developer, every factory, and every phone on earth.

The historical parallel isn't the space race. It's the semiconductor industry in the 1980s. Japan was going to "win" semiconductors. The US responded not by restricting its own companies, but by investing in SEMATECH, opening markets, and building the ecosystem that gave us Intel, Qualcomm, TSMC, and NVIDIA.

We need the AI equivalent of SEMATECH — a national commitment to American open-weight AI leadership, funded at scale, with clear rules that let companies compete globally instead of cowering from Friday afternoon government letters.

The window to act is narrowing. Every month that passes, another million developers adopt Qwen or DeepSeek as their default. Once the ecosystem locks in, it's very hard to reverse. Ask Microsoft about the browser wars — being better doesn't matter if the other guy is already installed on every machine.

My mom joined a Montana Survivalist Cult in 1988 so I got to see this belief system up close and personal.

I see some defending cults. 

You have no idea what you are up against.

items.title:
The solution is isolation by design.

→ Research agent: can browse the web, cannot touch private dat...
items.content:
The solution is isolation by design.

→ Research agent: can browse the web, cannot touch private data
→ Action agent: can access sensitive tools, has no internet
→ Credentials: stored in a vault, agents use them without seeing secrets
→ Sensitive actions: trigger a human approval card inside Slack, Teams, or WhatsApp
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Outlines isolation-by-design for agents: separate research and action agents, vaulted credentials, and human approval steps.
items.reasoning: Reasonably concrete agent-security pattern despite being part of a promotional thread; useful technical detail.
items.summary_html: Outlines isolation-by-design for agents: separate research and action agents, vaulted credentials, and human approval steps.
items.content_html:
The solution is isolation by design.

→ Research agent: can browse the web, cannot touch private data

→ Action agent: can access sensitive tools, has no internet

→ Credentials: stored in a vault, agents use them without seeing secrets

→ Sensitive actions: trigger a human approval card inside Slack, Teams, or WhatsApp

@realjohnmonarch I think it might look like it because

1) they speak really good English

2) they're close to British culture which is Anglo, and German culture, white American culture is of course highly Anglo and German

3) New York used to be New Amsterdam and a lot of the freedom mindset of America can be traced back to the Dutch colonists there, they didn't care about religion or skin color but they cared about trade (like most Americans)

But

4) modern Dutch culture is degrowth, anti-capitalist, anti-American, essentially suicidal and dishonest
